questions.question: Define the primary purpose of the `finally` block in Java exception handling.
keyPoints: The primary purpose is to provide a mechanism to execute cleanup code.
Examples of cleanup code include closing files or releasing system resources.

questions.question: Explain briefly the execution order of the `try-catch-finally` structure in Java.
keyPoints: If an exception is thrown, the `catch` block executes next.
The `finally` block always executes last, regardless of whether an exception was thrown.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the `finally` block with the `try-with-resources` statement in Java.
keyPoints: Both are used for resource management in Java.
The `finally` block requires manual resource management, while the `try-with-resources` statement provides automatic resource management.
The `try-with-resources` statement is more concise and easier to read than the `finally` block.

questions.question: Discuss the advantages of using the `finally` block in Java exception handling.
keyPoints: Ensures resource safety and reliability in Java applications.
Always executes, regardless of whether an exception was thrown.
Prevents resource leaks by releasing system resources in a timely manner.

questions.question: Explain with example scenarios where using the `finally` block is crucial for preventing resource leaks in Java.
keyPoints: File I/O operations: Ensuring files are properly closed, even if an exception occurs.
Database connections: Ensuring connections are properly closed, even if an exception occurs.
GUI component management: Ensuring components are properly disposed of, even if an exception occurs.
